coredata.dc:title: Insight into nanomaterials mediated dual-modal assay for food safety: Design principles and applications
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Gengli
Luo, Xueli
Jiang, Guoyong
Li, Yu
Dauda, Sa-adu Abiola
Li, Jiayi
Pi, Fuwei
coredata.dc:description:
Background Innovative methods are urgently needed to meet the requirements of high accuracy and reliability in food safety control. However, conventional nanoprobes based on single-signal output are easily susceptible to interference and possibly cause false or inaccurate results in complex matrixes. Fortunately, emerging dual-modal nanoprobes, holding two different signals with independent signal transductions, provide mutually checked analysis results to enhance detection reliability and avoid false results to improve the accuracy of results, simultaneously. Notwithstanding a few reviews have summarized the advantages and different applications of the dual-modal technique, there is currently lack a comprehensive review focusing on the design principles and construction strategies for developing dual-modal nanoprobes.
Scope and approach Based on systematic summary and comprehensive understanding on the achievements about dual-modal methods reported in recent five years, in this review, five types of dual-modal construction strategies are proposed according to the signal centers of dual-modal nanoprobes. Additionally, to further interpret the principles, the operations and detailed constructions of these dual-modal nanoprobes in food hazards analysis are introduced as examples. Finally, the perspectives and challenges about dual-modal nanoprobes are given based on the developments of advanced nanoparticles and devices.
Key findings and conclusions Five types of construction principles of advanced dual-modal nanoprobes are first proposed, in this review, based on the signal centers of dual-modal nanoprobes for comprehensively elaborating the dual-modal technology. More importantly, this review offers a novel and directional guidance for designing and constructing dual-modal nanoprobes particularly in food safety management.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Innovative methods are urgently needed to meet the requirements of high accuracy and reliability in food safety control. However, conventional nanoprobes based on single-signal output are easily susceptible to interference and possibly cause false or inaccurate results in complex matrixes. Fortunately, emerging dual-modal nanoprobes, holding two different signals with independent signal transductions, provide mutually checked analysis results to enhance detection reliability and avoid false results to improve the accuracy of results, simultaneously. Notwithstanding a few reviews have summarized the advantages and different applications of the dual-modal technique, there is currently lack a comprehensive review focusing on the design principles and construction strategies for developing dual-modal nanoprobes.

Based on systematic summary and comprehensive understanding on the achievements about dual-modal methods reported in recent five years, in this review, five types of dual-modal construction strategies are proposed according to the signal centers of dual-modal nanoprobes. Additionally, to further interpret the principles, the operations and detailed constructions of these dual-modal nanoprobes in food hazards analysis are introduced as examples. Finally, the perspectives and challenges about dual-modal nanoprobes are given based on the developments of advanced nanoparticles and devices.

Five types of construction principles of advanced dual-modal nanoprobes are first proposed, in this review, based on the signal centers of dual-modal nanoprobes for comprehensively elaborating the dual-modal technology. More importantly, this review offers a novel and directional guidance for designing and constructing dual-modal nanoprobes particularly in food safety management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety has become a central issue toward global public health due to uncertain hazards including biological, chemical, and physical contaminations (Hua, Yu, Liu, & Xianyu, 2021; Li, Tian, et al., 2022c; Luo et al., 2020). Thus, it is necessary to explore sensitive and efficient tools for monitoring food contaminations that may involve in the entire process of production, transportation, and storage, to ensure food safety. To this end, a variety of well-developed analytical techniques such as chromatography, spectroscopy, capillary electrophoresis, and immunoassay have been utilized as alluring protocols to ensure food quality. Despite the high accuracy of these conventional methods, the necessary of expensive equipment and professional operator highly limit their extensive applications over the entire food supply chain. Therefore, feasible, economic, and rapid analysis technologies are imperative in food safety monitoring.

Nanomaterials-based probes, i.e., nanoprobes, furnish new strategies and broad prospects in food quality control (Hua, et al., 2021; Lv et al., 2018; Zhang, Wang, et al., 2022). For example, due to the promising intrinsic optical and physicochemical properties of nanoparticles (Huang et al., 2018), nanoprobes are capable of selectively recognizing targets and then transforming these recognition events into various signal readouts, including thermal (Yan, et al., 2022), colorimetric (Li, Tian, et al., 2022c), electrochemical (Feng, et al., 2022), fluorescent (Bhamore, Jha, Park, & Kailasa, 2018; Jalili, Khataee, Rashidi, & Luque, 2019), and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (Wang et al., 2021), etc. However, majority of nanoprobes only possess single-signal readout, which is easily suffered from unstable signal and limited applications. For example, dark colored food substrate cannot be analyzed by a colorimetric signal. Similarly, a fluorescent signal lacks adaptive capacity for samples with auto-fluorescence (Ding, et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2022).

To solve the abovementioned issues in conventional nanoprobes, dual-modal detection technology is emerging, which can combine two signal transduction pathways into one method and compensate for the inherent limitations of each mode. Comparing with conventional single-signal method, the dual-modal technology has more detection flexibility by providing two alternative sensing modes, thus which can be adapted to different application scenarios according to different determination environments. For instance, a colorimetric and photothermal dual-modal nanoprobe has been explored to monitor food freshness, in which photothermal signal can still work when the food substrates were contaminated by the fish blood but colorimetric signal cannot work under this circumstance (Ding, et al., 2022). Moreover, the two signals not only can be mutually checked to enhance their reliability, but also used to adjust the analysis results to avoid false negative or positive results, thus improving the accuracy of results. In another example, SERS/electrochemiluminescence (ECL) dual-modal nanoprobe has been fabricated for Vibrio vulnificus (VV) sensing. The analysis results of VV in real samples obtained from SERS and ECL methods showed an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.998, the slope is approximately 1, while the intercept is approximately 0.), indicating that the two results can be mutually verified to improve the detection reliability and accuracy via avoiding false positive and negative results (Wei et al., 2021). To extensively develop and propose such advanced nanoprobes, there have been few reviews that tried to comprehensively summarize the dual-modal sensing technologies. For instance, Zhou et al. (Zhou et al., 2021) have summarized the different types of colorimetry/fluorimetry dual-modal detecting approaches. Alvarez-Puebla et al. (Alvarez-Puebla, Pazos-Perez, & Guerrini, 2018) have presented the fabrication strategies and applications of SERS-fluorescent encoded particles as dual-mode optical probes. Sun et al. (Sun, Li, Du, & Qi, 2023) have overviewed the integrated dual-modal optical sensor design strategies and their sensing assays for food safety. Notwithstanding, for such advanced and powerful technique, it is hard to get a prime interpretation over the design principles and construction strategies of dual-modal nanoprobes up to now.

In order to comprehensively interpret the dual-modal technique and provide a thorough understanding about dual-modal nanoprobes, in this review, the universal design principles and construction strategies for dual-modal nanoprobes are first summarized. Furthermore, to further interpret the principles, the operations and detailed construction strategies about dual-modal nanoprobes are introduced through employing food hazards analysis as examples. Basically, according to the signal centers in nanoprobes, we introduce the prime principles and construction strategies of dual-modal nanoprobes. Then, their applications and corresponding sensing mechanisms in food contaminants detection are elaborated. Finally, the challenges and perspectives for improving the analytical performance of dual-modal nanoprobes are discussed to promote a further application of these nanoprobes in food safety sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principles and strategies of dual-modal nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: Emerging developments in material science and nanotechnology provide promising avenues for fabrication of advanced nanoprobes and/or systems with dual signal output through employing various types of nanomaterials such as metal organic frameworks (MOFs), carbon dots (CDs), quantum dots (QDs), metal nanocluster, etc. Moreover, some chemical molecules are also cable of generating/regulating response signals in nanoprobes (Luo et al., 2023; Miao et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2022). Therefore, in this section, general design and construction principles for nanomaterials-modulated probes with dual output are introduced based on signal centers. Generally, the dual-signal nanoprobes as illustrated in Fig. 1 can be classified into five types according to the signal centers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single nanoparticle with dual signals (Type Ⅰ)
ce:section.ce:para: This type of approach refers to a nanoprobe that consists of one kind of nanoparticle capable of generating two different response signals simultaneously. Such methodology provides a very effective and simple strategy for the dual-modal detection of target analytes. Meanwhile, it does not require the introduction of additional signal entities, e.g., other specific nanomaterials or chemical molecules. As a result, this strategy can remarkably simplify the preparation process of dual-modal detection, as well as broaden their corresponding practical applications. Given these remarkable features, there has been increasing attention paid to the exploration of nanoparticles including CDs (Fu, et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2017; Mahmoud et al., 2022; Xiang et al., 2022; Yang, Wang, Wang, & Sun, 2020), QDs (Ahmed & Neethirajan, 2018; Dadkhah, Mehdinia, Jabbari, & Manbohi, 2020), metal nanoparticles (Pang, et al., 2021) and MOFs (Shang, Ding, Zhang, & Zhang, 2022) for constructing dual-signal outputs.

A typical way for Type I is to choose the precursors with property of at least two signals to synthesize a nanoparticle. For example, due to its unique tetrapyrrolic macrocycle motifs that exhibit both fluorescent and chromogenic properties, tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP), was chosen as an attractive signal generator capable of producing dual signals in both fluorescence and colorimetry. Thus, TCPP was utilized as porphyrin ligand to synthesize Zr-based MOF (PCN-222) for the colorimetric and fluorescent dual-modal detection of glyphosate (Luo et al., 2022). Additionally, some of the in-situ formed nanoparticles possess intrinsic dual signals, such as silicon-based nanoparticles (Jiang, Miao, Ding, & Lu, 2023a), polydopamine nanoparticles (Liu et al., 2020), and Prussian blue nanoparticles (PB NPs) and its analogs (Ding, et al., 2022; Xue et al., 2020). Modulating the generation or decomposition of these nanoparticles is another typical approach for Type I. For instance, silver-doped PB NPs with intrinsic colorimetric and photothermal signal can be decomposed by target trimethylamine, one of the characteristic indicators to food spoilage, thus achieving the dual-modal monitoring of shrimp and fish freshness (Ding, et al., 2022) (Fig. 2A). In contrast, target Fe3+ can induce oxidative etching of Au-Cu core-shell nanocube, and the reduction product Fe2+ can lead to in-situ formation of PB NPs, enabling the dual-modal detection of Fe3+ (Xue, et al., 2020). Besides, some noble metal nanoparticles, e.g., Au NPs or Ag NPs, possess high light extinction coefficients and exhibit high photothermal conversion efficiency, displaying dual signals output capacity. For instance, gold nanostars (AuNS) could be coated with silver under alkaline phosphatase modulation, causing changes in morphology and size. Such silver deposition not only causes a significant blue shift in the localized surface plasmon resonance of AuNS, but also decreases the photothermal conversion efficiency, enabling colorimetric and photothermal dual-modal sensing (Liu, Pan, et al., 2019) (Fig. 2B).

Type Ⅱ involves two or more kinds of nanomaterials to generate different response signals. Compared with Type Ⅰ, this strategy not only retains the beneficial merits of each nanomaterial, but also presents a viable option for accessing additional signal transduction channels to meet diverse analytical requirements through the flexible combination of two signal response centers (Huang et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2021). Generally, there are two construction methods to fabricate such nanoprobes based on the independence of the involved nanoparticles.

In the first case, dual-modal detection is achieved by employing two independent sensing nanoparticles. These nanoparticles operate independently in terms of their response reactions and signal transduction. Meanwhile, each nanoparticle recognizes single target or event, and their respective recognition processes were translated into output signals. This strategy can be regarded as the combination of two independent recognition processes by each nanoparticle. Specially, this strategy required analytes that are cable of dual functions, which can induce the signal responses of each nanoparticle. For instance, dual functions ascorbic acid can not only induced a photocurrent change of CdS/ZnO hollow nanorod arrays, but also led to a blue shift of the surface plasmon resonance peak from Au nanobipyramids@Ag, achieving photoelectrochemical and colorimetric dual-modal sensing for Microcystin-LR quantification (Wei et al., 2018) (Fig. 2C).

In the second case, the nanoprobes usually are prepared by two interrelated nanoparticles, which can be either physically mixed or chemically assembled together. These nanoparticles can interact with each other before or after adding the analyte. The common strategy for this case is through the construction of signal-interrelated nanoprobes, in which one of signal generation/regulation is directly regulated by the other signal. Concretely, analytes can specifically induce the response of one of nanoparticles for pre-decided signal and then this response event can trigger the generation or regulation of the other signal, thereby producing the related response signal between these two response nanoparticles. For instance, the fluorescence of CQDs can be quenched by gold nanorods (AuNRs) through surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET). Conversely, the etch of AuNRs by OH· can cause a decrease in the absorbance intensity of AuNRs, thereby recovering the fluorescence of CQDs. Moreover, the etching reaction can be effectively suppressed by cysteine, resulting in a change in both the absorbance intensity of AuNRs and the fluorescence of CQDs, endowing the dual-modal detection of cysteine (Wang, Liang, et al., 2018) (Fig. 2D).

Despite the high efficiency to establish dual-signals nanoprobes, such strategy for different nanoparticles based nanoprobes still remain some challenges that needing to be addressed. This strategy generally requires two separated synthesis steps to generate two kinds of nanoparticles for different response signals, resulting in the tedious multistep preparation and thus is not the idea choice for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single chemical molecule with dual signals (Type Ⅲ)
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from signals generated from nanoparticles, some chemical molecules are also capable of generating/regulating response signals. Basically, the molecular-modulated signal response approach can be divided into two types: (i) chemical molecules that possess inherent signals, which can be regulated through amount change or physical/chemical reaction; (ii) chemical molecules that do not possess signals but can be activated through redox or other reactions to generate response signal. Surely, these chemical molecules can be combined with nanoparticle to assemble nanoprobe and utilized as signal centers. Table 1 summarized signal outputs and corresponding principles for some representative chemical molecules.

Notably, as list in Table 1, some chemical molecules hold at least two signals simultaneously. This unique characteristic allows for the development of dual-modal nanoprobes by incorporating a chemical molecule with a nanoparticle. In such cases, the nanoparticle acts as the signal transducer and the chemical molecule serves as the dual-signal center. For some of chemical molecules without signal, they can be employed in oxidization to form oxidized products by integrating with nanozyme (nanomaterials with enzyme-mimicking activity). That is, these pre-generated signals from the oxidation of substrates can be regulated by analytes through modulating the amount of oxidation production (Li, Wei, et al., 2022). For example, a typical chemical molecule, 3, 3′, 5, 5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) which can be oxidized into oxTMB through donating the lone pair of electrons from its amino group by the reaction of hydroxyl radical. Due to the oxTMB with various signals, TMB can be directly integrated with nanozymes to construct several kinds of nanoprobes for dual-modal detection of glucose (Shen, Zhao, Zhu, & Wang, 2021) and Hg2+ (Zhi, Zhang, Li, Tu, & Lu, 2022). Similarly, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) (Li, Wei, et al., 2022; Sun, Wang, Zhao, Li, & Yang, 2016; Zhang, Liu, Han, Luo, & Li, 2019; Zhou et al., 2022), 2, 2′-azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline)-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) (Garcia-Leis, et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2022) and polydopamine (PDA) (B. Liu, Han, & Liu, 2016) are also utilized as the substrates to be oxidized by nanozymes to generate different signals for dual-modal detection. Additionally, other chemical molecules, such as curcumin (Miao, et al., 2018) and rhodamine B (Ma, Zhang, & Wang, 2021), which possess inherent dual-signals, can serve as signal centers for achieving dual-modal detection by regulating their own amount. Cyanine dyes, such as Cy3 (Li, Tian, et al., 2022c) and Cy5.5 (Wang, He, et al., 2022), can act as fluorescence and Raman reporters for achieving dual-modal detection through conformational switching.

Although single chemical molecules with dual-signals have great potentials in the fabrication of dual-modal nanoprobes, these chemical molecules may be suffered from low sensitivity due to their low signal intensity (Zhou et al., 2021). To improve the sensitivity of these chemical molecules based nanoprobes, two strategies can be adopted to overcome the low signal intensity: (1) Integrating chemical molecules with signal amplification carriers. In this approach, the nanoparticles are usually chosen as signal amplification carriers with the following considerations: (i) Nanoparticles serve as supporters for concentrating capture molecules and/or highly loading chemical molecules; (ii) The nanoparticles could bring forth a synergistic effect by combining catalytic activity, conductivity, and biocompatibility to accelerate the signal transduction. For instance, owing to curcumin with colorimetric and fluorescent signals, Miao et al. (Miao, et al., 2018) reported a dual-modal nanoprobe through loading curcumin to MoS2 nanoflowers, whereas MoS2 was used as the curcumin carrier to amplify the detection signal through quickly releasing curcumin from MoS2. (2) Constructing enzyme catalytic reactions. For example, enzyme cascade reactions allow for a significant enhancement in reaction efficiency through effective coupling of two enzyme-catalyzed reactions, resulting in improved overall performance. As an example, Shen et al. (Shen et al., 2021) reported the successful dual-modal sensing of glucose by using TMB as a chromogenic substrate and incorporating enzymes as signal amplification units within the cascade catalysis reactions. Notably, this approach resulted in significant signal amplification and enabled a photothermal and colorimetric dual-modal detection methods (Fig. 3 A). Meanwhile, all response signals are depended on the single chemical molecule, which can be seen as the different representations for the signal of chemical molecule in this strategy. Therefore, their response signals may be simultaneously interpreted due to the same response mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signals from different chemical molecules (Type Ⅳ)
ce:section.ce:para: Aside from Type Ⅲ utilizing single chemical molecule as signal center alone, another strategy for designing nanoprobes with dual signal output from chemical molecules involves incorporating two or more chemical molecules, wherein each chemical molecules serve as signal center. According to the different reactions between these chemical molecules after adding analytes into nanoprobes, different chemical molecules-based dual-signal nanoprobes can be classified into two categorizations, i.e., signal-interrelated and signal-independent nanoprobes.

In the signal-interrelated nanoprobes, analytes can change the response signal steamed from one of chemical molecules, and then this recognition process can directly regulate the signal resulted from the other chemical molecule. For instance, Co3O4 nanosheets with POD-like activity can oxide TMB to produce colorimetric signal. Meanwhile, its oxidization product can quench the fluorescence signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+ based on the principle of the inner filter effect (IFE), thus realizing a colorimetric and fluorescence dual-modal for H2O2 detection (Tan, et al., 2022) (Fig. 3B). Besides, electrochemical signal is also utilized to construct the dual-modal nanoprobe in this situation, in which redox reactions are commonly employed to assemble the electrochemical and colorimetric nanoprobes (Zhou et al., 2021). For example, polyhedral AuPd alloy nanoparticles can catalyze H2O2 to generate •OH, which can directly result in an amplified electrochemical signal. Simultaneously, TMB was catalyzed by the •OH to generate a colorimetric signal, enabling dual-modal sensing (Wang, Liang, et al., 2018).

In the later situation, the regulations of dual signal are independent without relationship, which also can be divided into two cases. Briefly, in the first case, each of chemical molecules can react with the nanoparticle to produce corresponding signals though using the same functional property of nanoparticle, where just the difference of the chemical molecules. Thus, such approach can simplify the optimization of detection conditions due to the same response mechanisms. For instance, a molecularly imprinted polymer nanozyme (MIL-101(Co, Fe)@MIP) with peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity can catalyze the reduction of H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Subsequently, the chemical molecule terephthalic acid and TMB can be oxidized by the •OH to produce fluorescent signal and colorimetric signal, respectively, thereby achieving the dual-modal detection of vanillin (Zhang, Wang, et al., 2022b). Similarly, reactive oxygen species that resulted from the catalytic reaction of dual enzyme-mimic nanozyme can oxidize the luminol or TMB to generate chemiluminescent and colorimetric signal, thus enabling dual-modal detection of chlorpyrifos (Chai, et al., 2023) (Fig. 3C).

In the second case, the nanoparticle possesses different functional properties, which can induce different chemical molecules to generate response signals, respectively. Compared with the use of same function, using different functions of nanoparticle have promising merits of high adaptability and reliability. From this view point, two individual chemical molecules can be sensitized by targets-triggered events with different response mechanisms to induce different signals to assemble dual-signal nanoprobe. For instance, target Hg2+ not only can trigger its catalase-like activity of Au/WO3 HNFs to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into O2, resulting in a pressure signal, but also can stimulate its peroxidase activity to oxidize TMB to generate colorimetric and photothermal signal, thereby allowing for triple-modal sensing of Hg2+ (Zhi, et al., 2022) (Fig. 3D).

The above-mentioned different chemical molecule based nanoprobes offer a new sight for designing nanoprobes for dual-modal instead of relying solely on the nanoparticles. However, such strategy usually requires at least two signal chemical molecules to be incorporated into the nanoparticle, which usually leads to difficulties in terms of cost or construction. To overcome these challenges, single chemical molecule with dual signals (Type Ⅲ) should be promising candidates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signals from nanoparticle and chemical molecule (Type Ⅴ)
ce:section.ce:para: In this strategy, nanoprobes for dual-modal sensing are achieved by incorporating a chemical molecule with a nanoparticle, where both the chemical molecule and the nanoparticle possess signals and thus act as signal centers. Moreover, there is an interaction between the nanoparticle and chemical molecules. Typically, this integration approach can be divided into two cases. In the first case, the signal of nanoparticle is directly modulated by analytes through changing the properties or numbers of nanoparticles without any regulation from chemical molecule. In such case, one of the most common strategies is designing the nanoparticles that not only possesses photothermal or fluorescent signal but also exhibit nanozyme activity to catalyze the substrate to generate signal. For instance, EMSN@Pt NCs exhibited both POD-like activity and fluorescence emission, thus it can oxide TMB to induce colorimetric signal. In the presence of Hg2+, which can not only react with Pt atom to decrease the fluorescence intensity, but also inhibit the POD-like activity of EMSN@Pt NCs to decrease the colorimetric signal (Shen et al., 2021). Besides, PCu@pAb nanoprobe with both photothermal properties and POD-like activity, was served as the signal element for constructing the dual-modal lateral flow immunoassays (LFA). Upon increasing the concentration of target Aspergillus flavus, the formation of the sandwich structure triggered by Aspergillus flavus also increased and efficient generation of the PCu@pAb-based colorimetric and photothermal readout (Liang, Cai, et al., 2022) (Fig. 4 A).

In the second case, the modulation of the nanoparticle signal is not directly influenced by the analyte itself, but rather by a chemical molecule. Specifically, the nanoparticles can recognize analytes and meanwhile transform the recognition events into modulation of chemical molecule signal. Such regulation events can adjust the signal of nanoparticles, thereby triggering the production of two interconnected response signals. The commonly used method for this case is to prepare nanoparticles that not only exhibit luminescence signal, but also possessed enzyme-mimicking activity to catalyze chromogenic substrate for inducing colorimetric signal. Meanwhile, the catalytic products can trigger the luminescence signal changes with interconnected response via inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. For example, single-atomic iron doped CDs (SA Fe-CDs) were exploited with oxidase-mimetic activity to catalyze TMB oxidation to produce the colorimetric signal. Meanwhile, the oxidation product of TMB also can quench the photoluminescence signal of SA Fe-CDs due to IFE. Moreover, phosphate ions can be used to inhibit the oxidase-mimetic activity of SA Fe-CDs through interacting with iron active sites. This not only reduced the colorimetric signal of TMB, but also restored the photoluminescence signal of SA Fe-CDs, enabling dual-modal detection of phosphate ions (Li, Wei, et al., 2022) (Fig. 4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of dual-modal nanoprobes for food safety detection
ce:section.ce:para: To further interpret the principles of dual-modal nanoprobes, the following section will be elaborated to detail constructions and operations through gradually introducing the evaluations of dual-modal nanoprobes on food hazards from common pesticides, ions sensing to pathogen, biotoxins, and food allergies analyses. Table 2 listed a summary of nanoprobes with dual signals for food hazards evaluation in different food matrices.

Pesticides are widely used to control pest and weeds in farming for increasing agricultural production. However, over-application and improper handling of pesticides can result in residue accumulation in crops, thus causing a growing threat to environment and human health (Li, Tian, et al., 2022c; Peng Liu, Zhu, et al., 2022e; Luo et al., 2022; Umapathi, Ghoreishian, Sonwal, Rani, & Huh, 2022). Concerns of the harmful effects of pesticide residues have driven attempts to explore sensitive and selective sensing methods for food safety control. Recently, a large number of nanoprobes with dual-signals sensing have been applied to detect pesticide residues in agricultural food, mainly focusing on enzyme activity inhibition-based methods (Cai, et al., 2020; Jiang, Yang, et al., 2023b; Jin et al., 2019; Li, Tian, et al., 2022c; Peng Liu, Zhu, et al., 2022e; Luo et al., 2023). For instance, cerium-based coordination polymer nanoparticles (CPNs(Ⅳ)) possess both oxidase-like mimetic catalytic activity and stimulus-responsive fluorescence property. Ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) can be hydrolyzed to generate ascorbic acid (AA) with the catalyzation of the enzymatic activity of acid phosphatase (ACP). When pesticides are present, it can restrain the enzymatic activity of ACP, recovering the catalytic TMB oxidation reaction and suppressing the fluorescent signal of the CPNs(III) resulting from the reduce of CPNs(Ⅳ) by AA. Therefore, dual-modal fluorescent and colorimetric detection of pesticide residue was achieved in unhulled rice and cucumber (Fig. 5 A) (Li, Tian, et al., 2022c). Besides the utilizing of enzyme-mimicking activity inhibition-based methods, Luo et al. (Luo et al., 2022) proposed an interesting method for ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-modal determination of glyphosate by using N-CDs@PCN-222 nanocomposites, which can coordinate with glyphosate to form a complex, leading to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs@PCN-222. Moreover, the intrinsic color of nanoprobes also can be triggered by glyphosate to transform under day light. The applicability of the proposed method was well conducted by the practical detection of glyphosate in real food samples.

Harmful ions, such as heavy metal ions, iodine ions, and nitrite ions, have emerged as severe threats to food safety, thus posing potential risks to human health through bioaccumulation. Due to intrinsic self-confirmation and self-adjustment, several dual-modal nanoprobes have been proposed to solve the issues mentioned above. For instance, CD-wrapped AuNP nanoprobes were prepared to fluorescently and colorimetrically detect Cu2+ and Hg2+ in tap water sample by the interaction between these two metal ions and CDs on the surface of AuNPs (Fig. 5B) (Li et al., 2021). In another work, the POD-like activity and fluorescent emission intensity of FA@Ag-Pt QDs could be exclusively altered by the targets I− to produce differentiable color changes, thus offering a way to the colorimetric and fluorescent dual-modal detection of I− in agricultural products (Tang et al., 2023). In addition, red emission carbon dots (r-CDs) based nanoprobes were prepared to colorimetric/fluorescent dual mode detection of nitrite in ham and bacon samples (Fig. 5B) (Yan et al., 2022).

During the past few decades, food pathogens have raised increasing concerns among the pubic due to their health-related problems, such as acute vomiting and abdominal pain. Therefore, it is urgent to explore an accurate, reliable and sensitive ways to detect foodborne pathogens in food products to ensure food safety. Over the years, dual-modal nanoprobes with inherent self-validation and self-correction have been utilized as promising tools for pathogens detection, often conjugated with recognition components (e.g., antibodies, aptamers, and peptides) to capture the target bacteria. For instance, to achieve accurate and reliable detection of Listeria monocytogenes, a fluorescence-colorimetric dual-modal nanoprobe was constructed based on the Fe-doped polydopamine (Fe@PDA)-based nanozyme, which possessed both POD-like activity and fluorescence emission ability. Both aptamer-functionalized Fe@PDA (apt/Fe@PDA) and vancomycin-functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles (van/Fe3O4) were able to bind with Listeria monocytogenes through the specific binding ability of the aptamer and vancomycin. After magnetic separation of the unbound apt/Fe@PDA through van/Fe3O4, the residual apt/Fe@PDA in the precipitation can not only act as a fluorescence signal, but also catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce a colorimetric signal, thereby realizing dual-modal sensing in water samples (Fig. 5C) (Shen, et al., 2022).

Biotoxins, particularly mycotoxins and bacterial toxins, can cause damage to human health. Therefore, the development of dual-modal nanoprobes for reliable and accurate detection of biotoxins is urgent for food safety control. Similar to the detection of food pathogens, aptamers and other bioactive substances are often conjugated for the construction of nanoprobes for dual-modal sensing biotoxins. For example, a tetrodotoxin (TTX) specific aptamer was labeled with cyanine-3 with fluorescence emission ability and Raman reporter property to utilize as the recognition element and signal center (Cy3-aptamer). It was then adsorbed onto the surface of AuNPs@MIL-101, resulting in quenched fluorescent signal and enhancing SERS signal. In the presence of target TTX, Cy3-aptamer would preferentially bond to the TTX to form Cy3-aptamer–TTX complexes, which would dissociate from the surface of AuNPs@MIL-101, leading to the recovery of fluorescence signals and the reduction of SERS signals. Based on the working mechanism mentioned above, a dual-modal nanoprobe combining fluorescence and SERS was applied for the detection of TTX in the nontoxic puffer fish and clam samples (Fig. 5D) (Li, Tian, et al., 2022c). Similarly, the staphylococcal enterotoxin B (SEB) aptamer was used as a recognition element, and Cy5.5 aptamer–modified AuNPs@MIL-101 were well applied for the dual-modal detection of SEB in milk samples (Wang, He, et al., 2022).

In addition, some proteins contain in food may result in food allergies, attaching the increasing demands to explore the reliable and accuracy techniques to assess the food allergens. For instance, the presence of target parvalbumin (PV), one of the main allergens in fish, would lead to dissemble the nanohybrid of the aptamer modified gold nanoparticles (AuNP-APT), complementary short chain modified gold nanoparticles (AuNP-CS1) and fluorescent dye labeled complementary short chain (FAM-CS2). The nanoprobes were performed on the principle that PV could dissemble the Au NPs nanohybrid and then release the FAM-CS2, leading to a color change of the AuNP colloids and a fluorescence variation of the FAM-CS2. Finally, the nanoprobes were successfully applied for the dual-modal detection of PV in real fish samples (Fig. 5E) (Wang, Li, Zhou, Qi, & Fu, 2020).

Besides the abovementioned food hazards, excessive intake of food additive or illegal additives also can seriously damage the human health. Thus, dual-modal based nanoprobes have widely used for monitoring those additives, such as bisulfite (Deng et al., 2023), vanillin (Zhang, Wang, et al., 2022b), indigo carmine (Refat, Hassan, Hassan, & El-Wekil, 2021) and rosiglitazone (Liang, Cai, et al., 2022). These nanoprobes with dual-signal output methods not only presented outstanding performances in merits of accuracy, reliability, and stability, but their functional complementarity and versatility also possessed great potentials in the future-oriented food safety control.

In this review, we proposed a prime interpretation about nanomaterials-mediated dual-modal approaches and summarized their sensing mechanisms along with construction strategies. The inherent self-validation and self-correction virtues of dual-modal nanoprobes offer more reliable and dependable results, providing a promising analysis platform for food safety control. Although dual-modal nanoprobes exhibit tremendous potential in the food quality control, there are still a few challenges and perspectives should be addressed to enhance the accuracy of analysis results and expand their ranges of application.
(1)
Nanoparticles development with multi-function and stable performance:

Dual-modal strategies highly rely on the intrinsic properties of employed nanoparticles. Although conventional nanoparticles have markedly improved the sensing performance of dual-modal techniques, most of them are easily suffered from drawbacks regarding the homogeneity and stability in diverse environments. Therefore, promising nanoparticles with good stability, perfect catalytic activity and excellent optical properties, should be continuously explored by designing of suitable synthesis scheme.
(2)
Further development of recognition molecules:

In order to extend the practical applications of dual-modal technique in complex systems, there are two critical aspects should be considered: excellent selectivity and sensitivity of nanoprobes is only one critical side, the other critical side is the abilities of target-specific recognition molecules. Various well-known sensing units such as aptamers, antibodies, and molecular imprinted polymers can be introduced and employed to significantly improve the sensing accuracy and further accelerate development of dual-modal techniques.
(3)
Integrating with and development of commercially-available portable devices:

So far, most of dual-modal approaches are mostly carried out in laboratories, while the practical applications especially for food hazards sensing needed in the whole food chain from farm to fork/plate require point-of-care-test devices. Thus, integrating with commercially-available portable devices such as portable barometers, personal glucose meter, pH meters and thermometers shall be a promising and challengeable direction of dual-modal techniques. Surely, such integration could highly promote the developments and actual applications of dual-modal techniques in scientific investigations and sensing analytes.

To conclude, nanomaterials mediated dual-modal techniques play an important role in food safety and we firmly believe that such technique will serve as promising platforms, offering novel approaches and technologies for food safety control.

Gengli Huang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, preparation. Xueli Luo: Writing – review & editing. Guoyong Jiang: Supervision. Yu Li: Supervision. Sa-adu Abiola Dauda: Supervision. Jiayi Li: Visualization. Fuwei Pi: Writing – review & editing.
